coredata.dc:title: Morphology and phase transformation of α-MnO2/MnOOH modulated by N-CDs for efficient electrocatalytic oxygen evolution reaction in alkaline medium
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Lin
Zhai, Xiuhui
Wang, Xiang
Pang, Xinle
Li, Jun
Li, Zhao
coredata.dc:description: Engineering non-precious electrocatalysts with excellent water oxidation performance is imperative for developing renewable and clean energy. Herein, we realize the simultaneous morphology and phase transformation from α-MnO2 nanorods, large-size MnOOH nanorods to MnOOH nanoparticles by adding different content of N-CDs in the synthesizing α-MnO2 process. Benefitting from the unique structures, synergistic effect between Mn-based oxides/oxyhydroxides and N-CDs, various active oxide species and large specific surface areas, the as-prepared Mn-based oxides/oxyhydroxides@CDs with different morphologies show excellent electrocatalytic performance for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) in 1.0 M KOH. Impressively, the large-size MnOOH@CDs0.2 display high electrocatalytic performance with the overpotential of 302 mV to reach to 10 mA cm−2 along with a Tafel slope of 51.3 mA dec−1. More importantly, by coupling the MnOOH@CDs0.2 with Pt/C for overall water splitting, a cell potential of merely 1.56 V is required to reach to 10 mA cm−2. Additionally, in the process of stability studies, the key role of the (111) crystal plane in the catalytic process is established. Meanwhile, the MnOOH@CDs0.2 also shows excellent stability for continuous 72 h chronopotentiometry test. The proposed strategy showed here represents a facile and suitable method for the simultaneous transformation of the morphology and phase of Mn-based oxides/oxyhydroxides with the assistance of N-CDs for efficient energy conversion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: α-MnO2
MnOOH
N-CDs
Morphology and phase transformation
Oxygen evolution reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of various electrocatalysts on different magnification (a) α-MnO2@CDs0.1, (b) α-MnO2@CDs0.15, (c, d) MnOOH@CDs0.2and (e) MnOOH@CDs0.25, (f) elemental mapping images (EDX) of MnOOH@CDs0.25.(g) XRD pattern of MnOOH@CDs0.25.
(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) Raman spectra ofα-MnO2@CDs0.1, α-MnO2@CDs0.15, α-MnO2@CDs0.2, MnOOH@CDs0.25, α-MnO2 and CDs.
XPS spectra of (a) survey scan, (b) Mn 2p, (c) O 1s, (d) C 1s, and (e) N 1s in MnOOH@CDs0.2.
(a) LSV polarization curves, (b) Tafel slopes, (c) double layer current vs scan rates plots, and (d) Nyquist plots of α-MnO2@CDs0.1, α-MnO2@CDs0.15, α-MnO2@CDs0.2, MnOOH@CDs0.25, α-MnO2 and CDs.

(a) Schematic diagram and (b) LSV polarization curves for overall water splitting. (c) chronopotentiometry measurement of the overall water splitting at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 of MnOOH@CDs0.2 || Pt/C couple.
(a–b) Representative TEM image of MnOOH@CDs0.2 on different magnification after long-term electrochemical tests. (c) The XRD pattern of MnOOH@CDs0.2 before and after prolonged CP tests.
Schematic illustration of α-MnO2@N-CDs or MnOOH@N-CDs with different morphologies.
The mechanism of transforming from α-MnO2 to MnOOH with the assistance of N-CDs.

head.ce:title: Morphology and phase transformation of α-MnO2/MnOOH modulated by N-CDs for efficient electrocatalytic oxygen evolution reaction in alkaline medium

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Xuzhou University of Technology, Xuzhou, 221018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Xuzhou University of Technology, Xuzhou, 221018, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Engineering non-precious electrocatalysts with excellent water oxidation performance is imperative for developing renewable and clean energy. Herein, we realize the simultaneous morphology and phase transformation from α-MnO2 nanorods, large-size MnOOH nanorods to MnOOH nanoparticles by adding different content of N-CDs in the synthesizing α-MnO2 process. Benefitting from the unique structures, synergistic effect between Mn-based oxides/oxyhydroxides and N-CDs, various active oxide species and large specific surface areas, the as-prepared Mn-based oxides/oxyhydroxides@CDs with different morphologies show excellent electrocatalytic performance for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) in 1.0 M KOH. Impressively, the large-size MnOOH@CDs0.2 display high electrocatalytic performance with the overpotential of 302 mV to reach to 10 mA cm−2 along with a Tafel slope of 51.3 mA dec−1. More importantly, by coupling the MnOOH@CDs0.2 with Pt/C for overall water splitting, a cell potential of merely 1.56 V is required to reach to 10 mA cm−2. Additionally, in the process of stability studies, the key role of the (111) crystal plane in the catalytic process is established. Meanwhile, the MnOOH@CDs0.2 also shows excellent stability for continuous 72 h chronopotentiometry test. The proposed strategy showed here represents a facile and suitable method for the simultaneous transformation of the morphology and phase of Mn-based oxides/oxyhydroxides with the assistance of N-CDs for efficient energy conversion.

ce:section.ce:para: Facing the crisis of energy scarcity and environmental pollution, plenty of efforts were devoted to pursuing clean and renewable energy for attenuating the reliance on the traditional fossil fuels [1,2]. Hydrogen is a promising energy carrier with the advantages of high energy density, clean and renewable [3,4]. Water splitting driven by electrical power is regarded as a potential technology for preparing H2 in a large scale, yet needs effective and stable electrocatalysts to achieve the oxygen evolution reaction (OER), a key half reaction in water splitting, which is thermodynamic uphill and kinetic sluggish compared with the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [5–8]. Currently, the Holy Grail of electrocatalysts toward OER are Ir/Ru-based oxides due to their excellent electrocatalytic performance [9]. However, their exorbitant price, scarce reserves and unsatisfactory stability seriously impeded their large-scale practical application [10]. With this in our consideration, it is imperative to develop low-cost and robust OER electrcatalysts for promoting the H2 production through water splitting.

Recently, economic transition metal oxides have captured tremendous attention due to their potential excellent electrocatalytic performance for OER [11]. Particularly, manganese, as one of the earth-abundant elements with non-toxicity and less expensive, is a promising candidate to meet the needs of artificial synthesis and has attracted a lot of attention [12,13]. Manganese oxides/oxyhydroxides were considered as potential OER electrocatalysts because of their excellent structural flexibility coupled with abundant electrochemical active sites, which could directly improve the electrocatalytic activity [14,15]. Among plenty of manganese oxides, MnO2, as the most commonly known MnOx, contains several polymorphs due to the arrangement pattern of the basic octahedral [MnO6] units, mainly including α-MnO2, β-MnO2, γ-MnO2 and δ-MnO2 [16,17]. The phase and morphology diversity of MnO2 is ascribed to the facile conversion among different valent manganese ions, being beneficial for the formation of defects, which can enhance the electrocatalytic OER performance [18]. Therefore the electrocatalytic OER performance of MnO2 is largely dependent on the conditions by which they are synthesized. With regard to MnOOH, it also showed potential OER activity due to its abundant active oxide species [19]. Nevertheless, the electrocatalytic performance based on MnO2/MnOOH still show a gap compared with another transition metals such as iron, cobalt, and nickel, which is mainly attributed to its unsatisfactory conductivity and poor stability [20]. Therefore, it is important and necessary to develop reasonable routes for constructing the Mn-based oxides/oxyhydroxides for enhancing the OER performance.

Carbon dots (CDs), as the semi-spherical and 0D nanomaterials, are easily available, low-cost, nontoxic, bio-compatibility. In addition, they also showed the advantages of large specific surface areas, supernal conductivity, feasible electron transfer ability and large electron storage ability, all of which endow them with great promise for the application in many fields, including sensors, imaging techniques, luminescent material, photo-/electro-catalysts and so on [21–23]. In our previous works, the addition of CDs into prepared MnO2 can improve the dispersity, facilitate the conductivity, increase the electrochemical activity area and enhance the stability, resulting in substantial enhancement in OER performance [24]. Our another works also showed that the morphology of NiMoP nanomaterials could been tuned with the assistance of CDs in the synthetic process of NiMoP [25].

Under our previous work coupled with above analysis, herein, we propose that the addition of CDs during the prepared MnO2 process might efficiently adjust the morphology and phase of MnO2 to improve the conductivity and stability of products, which boost the electrocatalytic performance for water oxidation. Fortunately, the morphology and phase transformation of MnO2 from α-MnO2 nanorods to large-size MnOOH nanorods were realized with the assistance of the different amounts of CDs. All the prepared samples showed better OER electrocatalytic performance compared with the raw α-MnO2, in which the nanorod-like MnOOH@CDs merely needs 302 mV to drive the oxygen evolution reaction to achieve the current density of 10 mA cm−2, and the Tafel slopes is as low as 51.3 mV dec−1. More importantly, by coupling the nanorod-like MnOOH@CDs with Pt/C for overall water splitting, a cell potential only 1.56 V is required to achieve 10 mA cm−2. This works shed bright light for developing low-cost and highly-efficient Mn-based oxides/oxyhydroides OER electrocatalysts by regulating morphology and phase with the assistance of N-CDs.

The N-CDs were prepared according to a reference with a few modifications [26]. In a typical process, 0.6 g dicyandiamide (DCDA) and 0.9 g citric acid (CA) were added into a mortar and grinded to be uniform. Then, the homogeneous mixture was heated to 170 °C at a rate of 10 °C min−1 and kept for 2 h under a continuous N2 flowing. After cooling naturally to room temperature, the samples were poured into distilled water and centrifuged at 8 000 rpm for 5 min Then the collection was further purified through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, the yellow N-CDs powders were obtained by freeze-dried.

0.632 g of KMnO4, 1.014 g of MnSO4·H2O and 0.2 g of N-CDs were added into 17 mL of deionized water and stirred for 10 min. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and kept for 6 h at 160 °C. After cooling the oven naturally to room temperature, the products were collected by a centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min and washed with deionized water for several times. The obtained samples were labeled as MnOOH@CDs0.2, another samples were obtained by varying the content of N-CDs from 0.2 g to 0.1 g, 0.15 g and 0.25 g, respectively, while keeping another conditions were unchanged, and the as-obtained samples were labeled as α-MnO2@ CDs0.1, MnOOH@ CDs0.15 and MnOOH@CDs0.25, respectively.

The morphologies of different samples were characterized by a transmission electron microscope using 200 kV instruments (TEM, JEOL-2100, Japan). Scanning electron microscope images were captured by a XL30 FESEM FEG microscope. The crystal structure and phase was established by using X-ray powder diffractometer with a Cu radiation source (λ = 0.15406 nm) (XRD, Rigaku UIV Japan). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas of different samples were measured by a physical adsorption apparatus and the pore-size distribution was calculated by the BJH method (Quadrasorb-S1, USA). The surface chemical composition and valence was determined by an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, JEOL JPS-9010, Japan). The thermal stability of samples was analyzed by a thermogravimetric analyzer (TA, TAQ50, USA).

All electrochemical measurements were carried out on an electrochemistry workstation (CHI-760E) with a standard three-electrode system in 1 M KOH. The pre-treated Ni-foam coated samples (1 cm * 1 cm), graphite rod, and Ag/AgCl electrode were employed as working electrode, counter electrode, and reference electrode, respectively. The polarization curves were collected by using the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) at a scanning rate of 5 mV s-1. iR drop was compensated at 95% for tests. The Tafel slopes were evolved from the LSV polarization curves with a scanning rate of 1 mV s-1. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted at an overpotential of 0.7 V in a range of frequency (1 Hz-1000 kHz) with 5 mA amplitude of current perturbation. The state-of-the-art IrO2 catalyst was also employed as the benchmarked electrocatalys. In order to prepare working electrode, 5 mg of catalysts was dispersed into the mixture with 950 μL of ethanol and 50 μL of Nafion solution (5 wt %), followed by sonicating for 60 min 10 μL of the prepared catalyst ink was uniformly coated on the Ni-foam treated with acetone for 4 h and 1 M HCl for 10 h, Then dried in the oven at 60 °C.

As schematically illustrated in Scheme 1 , various Mn-based oxides/oxyhydroxides were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method using KMnO4 and MnSO4·H2O as raw materials and adding different content of N-CDs. The transmission electron microscopy was applied to investigate the morphology of samples. As show in Fig. 1 a–c, the shape of different samples maintained rod unchanged except that the thickness increases over the amount of N-CDs, the diameter distributions of different rods were showed in the inset of Fig. 1a–c. As seen, the diameters are about 18 nm, 22 nm and 120 nm for α-MnO2@CDs0.1, α-MnO2@CDs0.15 and MnOOH@CDs0.2, respectively, After careful investigation (Fig. 1d), we can also find that the surface of MnOOH@CDs0.2 are rough, which can effectively facilitate the electron mobility between different rods [27,28]. Continually increasing the content of N-CDs, the morphology of samples showed irregular blocky structure (Fig. 1e). A further observation can also find that the plenty of N-CDs embedded on the surface of various Mn-based nanorods, manifesting the successful fabrication of Mn-based oxides/oxyhydroxides@CDs. The EDX images of Mn, O, C, and N elements were represented in Fig. 1f, the different elements uniformly were distributed throughout the whole samples, suggesting once again the composites were successfully constructed [29]. In order to determine the phase and structure of samples with the addition of different amount of N-CDs, the XRD pattern of various products were recorded in Fig. 1g. As seen, the XRD patterns of the samples, with the content of N-CDs ranging from 0.1 g to 0.15 g, were similar, which can match with α-MnO2 (PDF # 97-002-0227). A careful observation can also find that the (111) crystal plane of MnOOH became obvious, manifesting that the formation of mixed crystal phase with α-MnO2 and MnOOH. Interestingly, while the amount of N-CDs continually increased, the XRD patterns changed considerably, which were well consistent with MnOOH (PDF # 97-008-4949). The results combined with the analysis of TEM powerfully established that the CDs can not only effectively modulate the morphology but also transform the phase from manganese oxides to manganese oxyhydroxides.

By coupling the analysis of TEM and XRD with the previous reported, the mechanism of transforming from α-MnO2 to MnOOH with the assistance of N-CDs was proposed. The possible chemical reactions are showed as follows:
(1)
KMno
4
+
MnSO
4
→
MnO
2
(2)
MnO
2
+ N - CDs → MnOOH

In order to vividly explain the formation of large-size MnOOH, the diagrams was depicted as Scheme 2 , firstly the α-MnO2 was formed via a simple hydrothermal method by employing KMnO4 and MnSO4 as raw materials [30]. Subsequently, plenty of N-CDs were attached to the surface of α-MnO2 because of abundant of –OH and –NH2 on the surface of N-CDs [31]. Meanwhile, the α-MnO2 was reduced by N-CDs, which was attributed to the amounts of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-CDs and CC bonds in the carbon cores [32,33], then, newly formed α-MnO2 was adhered on the N-CDs by the –OH and –NH2. The α-MnO2 was again reduced by N-CDs with the same process. Finally, the large-size MnOOH was formed via the repeated attachment and reducation.

In order to investigate the effect of the content of N-CDs on functional groups within the materials, the FT-IR was carried out. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs was firstly represented, the broad peak located around 3421 cm−1 was assigned to the stretch vibration of O–H and N–H [34], the bands at 1710 cm−1 and 1658 cm−1 were ascribed to CO and CONH stretching vibrations [35], respectively. Moreover, the characteristic peak of O–H bending vibrations were pointed out to present at 1489 cm−1 [36], and C–N and C–O stretching vibrations were found to the peaks of 1299 cm−1 and 1181 cm−1 [37]. These results have manifested the N-CDs were successfully synthesized. The IR spectrum of α-MnO2 with no addition of N-CDs was recorded as a reference, the peaks at 718 cm−1, 591 cm−1, 522 cm−1 and 469 cm−1 were identified as the Mn–O stretching vibrations [38], the peak of 1616 cm−1 indicated to the existence of Mn–O–Mn [39], and O–H bending vibrations were assigned to the peaks of 1111 cm−1 [40]. When the N-CDs were added, we could find that the IR spectrum of as-prepared samples was nearly consistent with the one showed by α-MnO2 except that the peaks occurred a little shift, strongly suggesting that the Mn-based oxides/oxyhydroxides@CDs were successfully prepared. A careful observation found that the peak of around 590 cm−1 and 1660 became obvious and vague with the increase of the quality of N-CDs, respectively, which indicated that the decrease of Mn–O bonding and increase of Mn–O–Mn bonding, manifesting that the transformation from MnO2 to MnOOH [41]. The peak around 1111 cm−1 was split into three peaks, assigned to the γ-OH, δ-2-OH and δ-1-OH, respectively, further indicating that the phase transformation from MnO2 to MnOOH with the assistance of N-CDs [42]. Meanwhile, the Raman technique was employed to investigate the existence of the functional groups in different samples. Fig. 2b showed the Raman spectrum of as-obtained samples. The peaks around 633 cm−1 may originate from the v 2(Mn–O) symmetric stretching vibrations of [MnO6] octahedron. v 3(Mn–O) stretching vibrations in the basal plane of MnO6 sheet was generally located at around 569 cm−1 [43], where the peaks was disappeared in MnOOH@CDs0.2, implying that the octahedral crystalline was predominant with suitable the quality of N-CDs, and the irregular morphology is also beneficial for electrocatalysis [44]. And the peaks around 375 cm−1 and 173 cm−1 were attributed to the Raman-active modes in the Raman spectra of α-MnO2, the α-MnO2@CDs showed similar peaks with α-MnO2 but a slight shift, which originated from the synergistic effect between α-MnO2 and N-CDs [45], The characteristic peaks of MnOOH were located at 474 cm−1, 363 cm−1, 307 cm−1 and 265 cm−1 [46].

The XPS was applied to further confirm the surface chemical composition and valence state of MnOOH@CDs0.2. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the full-spectrum of MnOOH@CDs0.2 indicated that the samples consist of Mn, O, C, and N elements, in which C and N originated from the N-CDs, implying the CDs were successfully bonded to the surface of MnOOH via the abundant functional groups on the surface of N-CDs. Fig. 3b–e showed the fine-scan spectrum of Mn 2p, O 1s, C 1s and N 1s of MnOOH@CDs0.2, respectively. In Fig. 3b, the high-resolution spectrum of Mn 2p displayed two peaks at 642.21 and 653.80 eV, which were assigned to the binding energies of Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2, respectively [47]. The main peak suggested the existence of Mn3+, implying the uniform valence of manganese element in the MnOOH@CDs0.2, which was consistent with the analysis of XRD. In Fig. 3c, due to the various oxygen comes from CDs and MnOOH, the O 1s spectrum was split into many peaks at different binding energies, the characteristic peaks at 530.35 eV and 531.18 eV were ascribed to Mn–O and Mn–O–H [48], another peaks located at 529.73 eV, 531.77 eV, and 532.24 eV could be attributed to the following oxygen components: oxygen vacancies, C–O (sp2), and CO (sp) of N-CDs [49], all of which were consistent with the analytic conclusion of IR spectrum. For C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3d), four peaks were derived from the C species originated from CDs, which were ascribed to C–C (284.85 eV), C–N (288.70 eV), C–O (292.39 eV), and O–CO (295.23 eV) [50], respectively. In Fig. 3e, the N 1s spectrum uncovered the presence of only one types of nitrogen species, pyrrolic N at 400.16 eV [51], which was beneficial for boosting the electrocatalytic water oxidation [52].

Stimulated by uniform rodlike structure, facilitated electron transfer rate, synergistic effect between Mn-based oxides/oxyhydroxides and N-CDs and abundant active oxygen species, such Mn-based oxides/oxyhydrogenides@CDs are strongly desirable to equip with an excellent electrocatalytic performance. With this in mind, the electrocatalytic OER activity is evaluated on a standard three-electrode system in 1.0 M KOH. For comparison, the LSV polarization curves of α-MnO2, nickel foam (NF), and IrO2 are also provided. In Fig. 4 a, all prepared electrodes showed obvious oxidation peaks, which ranged from 1.3 V to 1.5 V, although the cathodic current of some prepared electrodes surpassed the 10 mA cm−2 at this potential ranges, there were no bubbles release from the electrodes surface, which was attributed to the Ni-foam oxidation of Ni2+ to Ni3+ [53–55], all fabricated electrodes displayed better electrocatalytic activity than α-MnO2, especially the MnOOH@CDs0.2, which even surpass the state-of-the-art IrO2, manifesting the suitable content of CDs can effectively improve the activity towards water oxidation. The MnOOH@CDs0.2 also exhibited a minimum onset potential (1.48 V), being 30, 60, 70, 80, 90, 110, and 130 mV lower than commercial IrO2 (1.51 V), α-MnO2@CDs0.15 (1.54 V), MnOOH@CDs0.25MnOOH@ (1.55 V), CDs (1.56 V), α-MnO2@CDs0.15 (1.57 V), MnO2 (1.59 V) and NF(1.61 V) (Fig. S1), respectively, implying the electrocatalytic MnOOH@CDs0.2 can smoothly start up water oxidation reaction [56]. In addition, for vividly comparing their OER activities, the overpotentials of the as-prepared electrocatalysts at 10 mA cm2 were exhibited with a bar diagram in Fig. S2. As depicted, the MnOOH@CDs0.2 showed excellent OER activity with an overpotential of only 302 mV to achieve 10 mA cm−2, which was 17, 68, 69, 73, 87, 91, 99 and 121 mV lower than those of α-MnO2@CDs0.15 (324 mV), MnOOH@CDs0.25 (345 mV), CDs (349 mV), α-MnO2@CDs0.15 (352 mV), MnO2 (357 mV) and NF (388 mV), respectively, almost compared with commercial IrO2 (284 mV). To our knowledge, the MnOOH@CDs0.2 showed the relatively lower overpotential at the current of 10 mA cm−2 compared with recently reported Mn-based/doped OER electrocatalyst (Table S1).

The Tafel slopes derived from LSV curves of corresponding electrocatalysts were represented in Fig. 4b, where the MnOOH@CDs0.2 showed smallest Tafel slope of 51.3 mV dec−1, which was smaller than another electrocatalysts and commercial IrO2, implying an available OER kinetic of MnOOH@CDs0.2 electrocatalyst [57]. Moreover, to further reveal the reason of the improvement of OER electrocatalytic activity, the ECSAs based on double layer-capacitance (Cdl) were also calculated (Fig. 4c). As shown in Fig. 4c, the Cdl for MnOOH@CDs0.2 was 5.06 mF cm−2, which was larger than another samples, indicating the CDs can improve the active surface area of catalyst. Fig. 4d recorded the EIS curves of each electrocatalyst. All the Mn-based oxides/oxyhydroxides@CDs exhibited smaller curvature than α-MnO2, suggesting the better capacity of promoting charge transfer, which was attributed to effective contact among each Mn-based oxides/oxyhydroxides@CDs nanorods and the synergistic action between Mn-based oxides/oxyhydroxides and CDs [58].

Inspired by the excellent OER activity, the MnOOH@CDs0.2 were also highly desirable to show excellent performance for overall water splitting, a two-electrode system, employing MnOOH@CDs0.2 as an anode and Pt/C as a cathode, was equipped for investigating electrocatalytic performance toward water splitting in 1.0 M KOH (Fig. 5 a). Abundant O2 and H2 bubbles at different electrodes can been clearly observed during the tests operation. More importantly, the MnOOH@CDs0.2 ‖ Pt/C electrode couple only needs 1.56 V to reach to 10 mA cm−2 (Fig. 5b), even lower than commercial IrO2 ‖ Pt/C couple (1.58 V). Apart from the electrocatalytic activity, the long-time stability is another key parameter for evaluating electrocatalytic performance. As depicted in Fig. 5c, the long-term chronopotentiometry technique was employed for assessing the stability of MnOOH@CDs0.2. The potential can maintain for more than 70 h with a negligible vibration, implying the as-obtained larger-size MnOOH@CDs0.2 nanorod has a potential application in overall water splitting [59].

To further evaluate the stability of the morphology and phase of MnOOH@CDs0.2 and illuminate the active crystal plane in the catalytic process, the TEM and XRD of the sample after long-term operation were carried out. As showed in Fig. 6 a, the TEM images of MnOOH@CDs0.2 catalyst after long-term CP tests maintain the structure of nanords, inflecting that the high structure stability. After careful investigation in Fig. 6b, we could also find the size of partial nanorods became smaller than original one, resulting from the agglomeration. As shown in Fig. 6c, the XRD peak at the degree of 26° is corresponded to the (111) crystal plane, which is apparently weaker than that before electrochemical test, significantly inflecting that the (111) crystal plane plays a key role in the OER catalytic process. A careful observation can find that the locations of XRD peaks after long-term test were nearly same with those of initial one, suggesting the phase were still unchanged.

In summary, we reported a facile hydrothermal method for the morphology and phase transformation of Mn-based oxides/oxyhydroxides from α-MnO2 to MnOOH with assistance of N-CDs. Systematically studying the role of N-CDs played in the morphology and phase transformation and the electrocatalytic OER performance of as-prepared various catalysts. The obtained large-size MnOOH@CDs0.2 nanorod showed excellent OER activity with an overpotential of merely 302 mV to achieve the current density of 10 mA cm−2 and a smaller Tafel slopes of 51.3 mA dec−1. Importantly, by coupling the MnOOH@CDs with Pt/C as anodic and cathodic electrode, the equipment only needs 1.56 V to drive the overall water splitting to reach the current density of 10 mA cm−2. Additionally, in the process of stability studies, we found that the (111) crystal plane played an important role in maintaining high electrocatalytic activity. More importantly, the MnOOH@CDs also exhibited excellent stability. After 72 h chronopotentiometry test, the variation of potential was nearly ignored and the morphology and phase of MnOOH@CDs still maintained. We showed a facile method for promoting electrocatalytic OER performance by transforming the morphology and phase of caltalyst with the assistance of CDs. This work may open a window for 0D nanomaterial CDs applied in electrocatalytic water oxidation performance.

The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors and all authors havegiven approval to the final version of the manuscript.Authors contributions as follows:Dr. L. Tian, Dr. L. Zhao and Ms X. H. Zhai jointly conceived and designed the study. Dr. L. Tian, Ms.X. H. Zhai, X. Wang, X. L. Pang, and J. Li performed together theexperiments. Dr. L. Tian, Dr. L. Zhao wrote the paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by The Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (19KJB150019), Youth Science and Technology Talents Enrollment Project of the Jiangsu Association of Science and Technology, We also thanked the Analysis and Testing Center, School of Materials and Chemical Engineering of Xuzhou University of Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2020.135823.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: PdMo bimetallene for oxygen reduction catalysis
Nature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.C. Luo, Z.L. Zhao, Y.L. Zhang, Y.J. Sun, Y. Xing, F. Lv, Y. Yang, X. Zhang, S. Hwang, Y.N. Qin, J.Y. Ma, F. Lin, D. Su, G. Lu, S.J. Guo, PdMo bimetallene for oxygen reduction catalysis, Nature 574 (2019) 81.

Int. J. Hydrogen Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Ion-Ebrasu, B. G. Pollet, A. Spinu-Zaulet, A. Soare, E. Carcadea, M. Varlam, S.Caprarescu, Graphene modified fluorinated cation-exchange membranes for proton exchange membrane water electrolysis, Int. J. Hydrogen. Energy 44 (2019) 10190.
